metadata.title: Connecting Action Semantics and Human Motion using Fuzzy Qualitative Kinematics

language.summary: Technical intent is recoverable, but grammar, awkward phrasing, and inconsistent terminology materially reduce clarity.

abstract.summary: The abstract states the motivation and high-level approach clearly, but results are vague and some claims are broader than what the paper supports.

structure.summary: The paper follows a mostly standard research-paper skeleton, but lacks a standalone Discussion section and places limitations after the conclusion.

related_work.summary: Coverage is directionally relevant, but the framing uses duplicate or task-misaligned citations and does not fully situate fuzzy or uncertainty-aware prior work.

novelty.summary: FQTs appear plausibly novel as a fuzzy extension of intermediate motion representations, but system-level novelty and the role of D-ANFI are only partially established.

methods.summary: The central idea is interesting, but the D-ANFI/fuzzy-learning component is underspecified and the formal presentation contains inconsistencies that hinder verification and reproduction.

math_symbols.summary: Equation numbering is present, but several symbols, operators, and token labels are inconsistent or undefined.

experiments.summary: The evaluation spans multiple tasks and benchmarks, but protocol ambiguity, incomplete baseline fairness, and missing statistics weaken empirical support.

results_claims.summary: The results support competitiveness on several metrics, but not the paper's broader claims of uniform superiority.

discussion.summary: The paper includes limitations, but interpretation and concluding claims are stronger than the evidence currently justifies.

figures_tables.summary: Artifacts are mostly integrated, but there are captioning and label-consistency problems, including a mislabeled quantitative table.

references.summary: Bibliography coverage is broad, but integrity is weakened by duplicates, malformed DOI formatting, and an unresolved citation placeholder.

citation_ethics.summary: No strong evidence of self-citation abuse is visible from the paper alone, but bibliography hygiene issues create moderate citation-integrity risk.

consistency.summary: The paper's high-level story is coherent, but method/table/token inconsistencies and unsupported summary claims materially reduce trust.

issues.title: Core D-ANFI and fuzzy-learning mechanism is underspecified
issues.description: The paper states that D-ANFI is a novel component and highlights it as a central contribution, but the evidence supports only a generic description of fuzzy inference layers rather than a precise, reproducible algorithm. The paper states that the system learns fuzzy rules and membership functions, but the evidence does not clearly support how rules are parameterized, how the fuzzy branch interacts with the DNN branch, or how D-ANFI is integrated into the final unified model. This leaves insufficient evidence to verify the technical contribution at the core of the paper.

evidence.excerpt: Third, we propose a dual-branch adaptive neuro-fuzzy inference (D-ANFI) block.
Fuzzy logic inference consists of the following four parts... 1) Fuzzifier 2) Knowledge base 3) Fuzzy inference 4) Defuzzifier
Our model employs a transformer-based variational autoencoder (VAE) network... encoders Ef, Em, and Et... decoder Dm...

issues.suggested_fix: Provide a complete algorithmic specification of D-ANFI, including branch inputs/outputs, rule structure, membership updates, branch fusion, and where the component is used in the final training and inference pipeline. Add an explicit ablation isolating D-ANFI from the rest of the system.

issues.title: Equations and notation contain mismatches that obstruct technical verification
issues.description: The paper presents a large formal apparatus for the token families, but the evidence supports several notation errors and mismatches: FJLAT is labeled as FLLAT in one equation, FLVT indices are inconsistent, some operators are undefined, and root/reference-vector notation is unclear. The paper states formal token definitions, but the evidence only partially supports a mathematically consistent specification.

evidence.excerpt: The crisp angle and FJLAT state... are determined using eq (10) and (11)... FLLAT... = μθ(...)
FLVT(j,k,†)_t = μv(v(j,†)_t)
Angle equations use bracket notation inside arccos without clearly defining the operator.

issues.suggested_fix: Audit all equations and symbol definitions, publish a notation table, and ensure every token name and argument list is consistent across prose and equations.

issues.title: Token names are inconsistent across the method, tables, and ablation study
issues.description: The method defines 11 token families using one nomenclature, but Table I and the ablation section use a different set of abbreviations without mapping them back to the formal definitions. The paper states that these artifacts describe the same representation family, but the evidence does not fully support traceability between the method and empirical sections. This substantially weakens confidence that the reported ablations and prompt analyses evaluate the exact formal objects defined earlier.

evidence.excerpt: There are 11 FQTs comprising ... FLLAT, FJLAT, FJJAT, FJJDT, FLJDT, FLLDT, FJDT, FLDT, FLADT, FJVT, and FLVT.
FJAT, FPAT, FRDT, FDT, FPRDT, FPDT, FLAT, FLOT, FJVT
F1 considers FJAT, FPAT, FJDT, and FRDT. F2 considers FDT, FPRDT, and FPDT. F3 considers FLAT, FLOT, and FJVT.

issues.suggested_fix: Use one authoritative token glossary across the paper or add a one-to-one alias map that explains how all table/ablation labels correspond to the formal token definitions.

issues.title: Preprocessing and extractor descriptions are internally inconsistent
issues.description: The paper reports conflicting sequence rates and token counts across sections. The paper states that sequences were resampled at 30Hz when constructing the token base, but later states that all sequences were resampled at 15Hz for training, while the dataset description separately mentions 20 fps. It also states both 11 FQTs and ten extracted token types. The paper states a fixed experimental protocol, but the evidence does not consistently support one authoritative setup.

evidence.excerpt: we then aligned the axes and resampled the sequences at a rate of 30Hz
All the sequences were resampled at a rate of 15Hz
The FQTE module... extracts ten different types of FQTs

issues.suggested_fix: Publish a single authoritative preprocessing description, including final fps, extraction stage, and token inventory. Reconcile the 10-versus-11-token and 30Hz-versus-15Hz inconsistencies.

issues.title: Evaluation protocol is ambiguous and may blur validation and test usage
issues.description: The paper reports that training used the training sets and the held-out test sets were used for validation. The paper states benchmark results as final evidence of superiority, but the evidence does not clearly separate validation, model selection, and final test reporting. This creates insufficient evidence that the comparison protocol is free from evaluation leakage or optimistic tuning.

evidence.excerpt: We utilized HumanML3D and KIT-ML training sets for training and held out test sets for validation

issues.suggested_fix: Clarify train/validation/test separation, state whether test splits influenced checkpoint selection or hyperparameter tuning, and rerun evaluation with a clean validation split if needed.

issues.title: Claims of broad superiority exceed the metric-level evidence
issues.description: The paper states that the method outperforms existing schemes broadly across tasks and datasets, but the evidence supports a more mixed picture. The paper does report strong scores on several metrics, especially some FID and multimodality settings, but the evidence does not consistently support best performance on every reported metric or dataset. The conclusion and abstract therefore overstate what the tables directly justify.

evidence.excerpt: Extensive experiments... validate the superiority of our approach over existing schemes.
HumanML3D motion generation Top-2 R-Precision: Ours 0.675 vs MotionGPT 0.681; KIT-ML Top-1: Ours 0.397 vs T2M-GPT 0.416.
The rigorous evaluation against SOTA methods underscores the superiority in motion quality, diversity, multimodality, text-motion correspondence, motion realism...

issues.suggested_fix: Rephrase summary claims to specify exactly which tasks and metrics show wins, and distinguish best results from competitive or mixed results.

issues.title: Baseline fairness and statistical support are insufficient
issues.description: The paper compares against many baselines, but several table cells are missing, some qualitative results are described as coming from published articles rather than controlled reruns, and no confidence intervals or statistical tests are reported for quantitative or user-study comparisons. The paper states strong comparative conclusions, but the evidence does not fully support fair, uncertainty-aware comparison across methods.

evidence.excerpt: Qualitative results for the baseline methods were collected from the respective published articles.
Multiple baseline entries are missing ('-') across tasks/metrics.
The figure reports preference percentages, but no error bars, significance tests, or confidence intervals are provided.

issues.suggested_fix: Separate reproduced numbers from imported numbers, add uncertainty estimates and significance testing, and limit claims to settings with directly comparable evidence.

issues.title: Reference list contains duplicates, malformed entries, and an unresolved placeholder citation
issues.description: Bibliography integrity is materially weakened by duplicate entries, inconsistent formatting, and an unresolved '[REF]' placeholder in the main text. The paper states a broad and careful prior-work grounding, but the evidence only partially supports clean citation hygiene. This is both an editorial and a scholarly-integrity concern because it affects attribution and traceability.

evidence.excerpt: we combine fuzzy logic inference with artificial neural network, and propose an adaptive fuzzy neural network [REF].
Both entries cite 'Bridging the gap between human motion and action semantics via kinematic phrases'.
Both entries cite 'Generating diverse and natural 3d human motions from text'.

issues.suggested_fix: Deduplicate the bibliography, resolve '[REF]', normalize DOI fields, and ensure one canonical citation exists for each work.

issues.title: Related-work positioning is weakened by duplicate and task-misaligned citations
issues.description: The paper positions FQTs against prior intermediate motion representations, but the evidence supports duplicate citation of the closest kinematic-phrases prior and reliance on some adjacent-task references (e.g., pose estimation or action recognition) without clearly separating direct predecessors from neighboring background. The paper states a clear novelty gap, but the evidence only partially supports that the related-work framing is precise enough to establish it.

evidence.excerpt: The section groups posebits, action-recognition motion cues, and kinematic phrases into one representation narrative.
The closest prior appears twice in the bibliography.

issues.suggested_fix: Rewrite the related-work section to separate direct semantic-motion representation predecessors from adjacent tasks, and explicitly compare FQTs against the closest prior using one canonical citation.

issues.title: Paper organization and prose reduce readability and traceability
issues.description: The paper retains a standard high-level structure, but readability is reduced by grammar problems, missing standalone Discussion, limitations appearing after the conclusion, and weak artifact linkage in places. The paper states its contributions clearly at a high level, but the evidence only partially supports publication-ready organization and prose quality.

evidence.excerpt: The D-ANFI block learn two data representations in parallel... integrating FQTs and D-ANFI block a unified unified motion understanding system...
VII. Conclusion is followed by VIII. Limitations and Future Work.
Figure 6 appears with a terse caption and weak explanatory linkage in the surrounding text.

issues.suggested_fix: Perform a language-editing pass, add a dedicated Discussion section, move limitations before the final conclusion or integrate them into the discussion, and strengthen figure/table linkage.

red_flags: Core method description is underspecified and internally inconsistent, limiting reproducibility.
Validation-versus-test wording is ambiguous, creating uncertainty about evaluation hygiene.
Token naming mismatches across method, tables, and ablations break traceability from definitions to results.
The bibliography contains duplicate entries and an unresolved '[REF]' placeholder citation.
Summary claims in the abstract and conclusion are stronger than the metric-level evidence currently supports.
strengths: The paper addresses an important problem: bridging raw motion and action semantics with a more interpretable intermediate representation.

FQTs are a plausible and potentially useful extension of prior symbolic or qualitative motion representations because they attempt to encode uncertainty and dynamics rather than only thresholded facts.
The evaluation spans multiple tasks, two standard benchmarks, qualitative examples, a user study, and ablation-style analysis.
The manuscript includes an explicit limitations section rather than omitting caveats entirely.
weaknesses: The central D-ANFI/fuzzy-learning contribution is not described precisely enough to verify or reproduce.
Notation, token definitions, and preprocessing details are inconsistent across sections.
Experimental claims are weakened by protocol ambiguity, incomplete baseline comparability, and missing significance testing.
Bibliography integrity and editorial quality require substantial cleanup before the paper can be considered publication-ready.
Language and structure issues materially hinder confident technical assessment.
questions_for_authors: What is the exact implemented definition of D-ANFI, including rule learning, membership updates, branch fusion, and where it enters the final unified model?
Can you reconcile all token names used in the method, Table I, and Table IV with a single authoritative glossary?
What is the correct preprocessing pipeline used in the final experiments, including fps, extraction stage, and train/validation/test split usage?
Which baseline numbers were reproduced under a matched protocol, and which were imported from prior papers?
Can you provide repeated-run statistics and significance testing for the main quantitative and user-study results?
Which components are the true novelty of the paper: FQTs, D-ANFI, the unified model, or the prompt-generation mechanism?
executive_summary: The main positive is the paper's representation idea: fuzzy qualitative tokens are a plausible attempt to bridge action semantics and raw motion with a more interpretable, uncertainty-aware intermediate layer. However, the current manuscript has substantial weaknesses in technical specification, internal consistency, and empirical rigor. The core D-ANFI/fuzzy-learning contribution is underspecified; notation and token naming are inconsistent across the method, tables, and ablations; preprocessing and evaluation details conflict across sections; and broad superiority claims are stronger than the metric-level evidence currently supports. Combined with bibliography-integrity problems and notable language issues, these concerns reduce confidence in the paper's readiness despite the underlying idea being potentially interesting.
